coredata.dc:title: A portable instrument-assisted and dual-model sensor array with distinguished color change for the point-of-care identification of tetracycline antibiotics

coredata.dc:description:
Background The development of a facile sensor array for the identification of multiple tetracyclines (TCs) is highly promising, considering their similar chemical structures and the associated challenges to public health and food safety. Herein, we explored a dual-model sensor array (ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric method) for detection and identification of multiple TCs.
Results Three types of ratiometric fluorescent probes based on blue carbon dots (Mg,N-CDs): Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G, were synthesized as sensing elements. Based on a multi-channel strategy involving the changed signals of the sensing elements, all three probes were able to generate multi-signal fluorescence responses. Under a 365 nm UV lamp, these fluorescence responses were accompanied by obvious color changes (blue to green, blue to red, yellow to red), providing an efficient strategy for on-site quantitative analysis of TCs, including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), metacycline (MTC), doxycycline (DOX), and doxycycline hyclate (DTC). A handheld portable 3D-printed platform was also constructed to further enhance the convenience and practicality of detection. This platform utilized the unique ratiometric fluorescence and visual responses of the probes to generate different “fingerprints” for each TC. Effective detection and identification of TCs and their mixtures were achieved by linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA).
Significance The sensitivity of the sensor array based on color change of ratiometric fluorescence was much higher than that of the conventional colorimetric sensor array. Thus, this method not only enriches the techniques for detection of TCs, but also introduces a low-cost, high-performance, dual-model detection tool for on-site analysis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of a facile sensor array for the identification of multiple tetracyclines (TCs) is highly promising, considering their similar chemical structures and the associated challenges to public health and food safety. Herein, we explored a dual-model sensor array (ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric method) for detection and identification of multiple TCs.

Three types of ratiometric fluorescent probes based on blue carbon dots (Mg,N-CDs): Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G, were synthesized as sensing elements. Based on a multi-channel strategy involving the changed signals of the sensing elements, all three probes were able to generate multi-signal fluorescence responses. Under a 365 nm UV lamp, these fluorescence responses were accompanied by obvious color changes (blue to green, blue to red, yellow to red), providing an efficient strategy for on-site quantitative analysis of TCs, including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), metacycline (MTC), doxycycline (DOX), and doxycycline hyclate (DTC). A handheld portable 3D-printed platform was also constructed to further enhance the convenience and practicality of detection. This platform utilized the unique ratiometric fluorescence and visual responses of the probes to generate different “fingerprints” for each TC. Effective detection and identification of TCs and their mixtures were achieved by linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA).

The sensitivity of the sensor array based on color change of ratiometric fluorescence was much higher than that of the conventional colorimetric sensor array. Thus, this method not only enriches the techniques for detection of TCs, but also introduces a low-cost, high-performance, dual-model detection tool for on-site analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The abuse of antibiotics has triggered serious risk to human health and ecological balance, which have become significant issues that are increasingly alarming to the public [1–3]. In China, antibiotics have recently been classified in the ‘The List of Key Controlled Emerging Pollutants (2023 Edition)' [4]. As the second most used antibiotics in the world, tetracyclines (TCs), including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), metacycline (MTC), doxycycline (DOX) and doxycycline hyclate (DTC) etc., are widely used in medicine, agriculture and animal husbandry [5–7]. The large-scale usage of TCs has triggered multiple environmental challenges [8]. Furthermore, exposure to high concentrations of TCs can lead to severe adverse health effects, including allergic reactions, bacterial resistance development, and hepatotoxicity. [9,10]. Hence, the environmental health issues arising from TCs have garnered significant global concern. Notably, an increasing number of distinct TCs have emerged. These TCs share similar structures and antibacterial functions, while exhibiting different side-effects on the ecological environment and human health [11]. Therefore, the identification and risk assessment of TCs are of great importance. To date, significant efforts have been dedicated to developing methods for the discrimination of TCs. High-performance liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (HPLC/MS) has emerged as a pioneering approach, offering superior resolution and accuracy [12]. Despite rapid progress in their applications, there are still significant challenges to consider and address in practical applications due to the complexity of procedures, high costs, and time-consuming analysis. Thus, this highlights the need for a method that is fast, accurate, affordable, economical, and suitable for on-site identification of multiple TCs.

The conventional chemosensors face challenges in simultaneously identifying similar analytes [13]. Sensor arrays that mimic artificial olfactory systems have become a powerful tool for the identification and detection of analytes with similar structures, overcoming the limitations of conventional optical sensors based on the lock-and-key strategy for detecting a specific analyte [14]. Especially, fluorescence sensor arrays based on the design of cross-reactive sensor elements have been extensively explored and have demonstrated remarkable achievements in developing robust detection strategies for various analytes. Wei et al. constructed a sensor array using two luminescent metal-organic frameworks (Zr-BUT-12 and Ga-MIL-61) for the rapid discrimination of six types of bisphenol contaminants [15]. Zhou et al. proposed a fluorescence sensor array for detecting PPs based on metal-ion-regulated gold nanoclusters via an indicator-displacement assay [16]. Despite rapid progress in the applications of fluorescence sensor arrays, the practical applications are restricted by external interference and their lack of portability. Most of the earlier works rely on a single fluorescence peak as the signal unit [17,18]. These sensor arrays are often influenced by instrument noise and environment. In contrast, the introduction of ratiometric fluorescence as the dual-signal unit to the sensor array can avoid false signals from background and improve the sensitivity and accuracy for the detection [19–21]. Therefore, it is crucial to design a rapid and intelligent sensor array that utilizes ratiometric fluorescence signals as the reporting mechanism.

Up to now, great attention has been paid to constructing new, robust sensor arrays based on ratiometric fluorescence to enable the sensitive identification of multiple TCs [22–26]. Liu et al. designed a fluorescent nanoprobe (2-aminoterephthalic acid-anchored CdTe quantum dots with Eu3+ coordination) featuring triple emissions to fabricate a ratiometric fluorescence sensor array for identifying multiple TCs [24]. Yuan et al. synthesized a 7-hydroxycoumarin-3-carboxylic acid-embedded Eu-MOF (HC@Eu-MOF) material to construct a ratiometric fluorescence sensor array for the sensing and discrimination of multiple TCs [25]. Zhu et al. reported a study of ratiometric trace detection and precise discrimination of TCs in dual-responsive luminescent MOF hybrids [26]. Nevertheless, development of sensor arrays based on ratiometric fluorescence for identification of multiple TCs is still in its early stages. The distinguished change of fluorescence color in each unit has not been employed to design a colorimetric sensor array, which would enable more significant and intuitive identification of multiple TCs and offers the possibility to develop the real-time and on-site method. Notably, the fluorescent color images can be acquired by a smartphone camera, and then the image data are turned into RGB values (red, green, and blue) using a smartphone app [27,28]. With portable devices, the RGB values can be converted into spectral data and are further analyzed using pattern recognition methods such as linear discriminant analysis (LDA) [29–31]. The colorimetric method provides a straightforward and visual means of detecting analytes, making it accessible for users without specialized equipment. By combining ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric methods, the dual-modal sensor owns the strengths of both methods, resulting in improved selectivity, robustness, and versatility for detecting target analytes. Therefore, the development of dual-model sensor array (ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric method) for identification of multiple TCs is highly promising.

Inspired by these advancements, we designed three simple yet efficient multi-channel ratiometric fluorescence probes (Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G) as the sensing elements for the effective detection and identification of TCs. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , by combining the excellent fluorescence properties of Mg,N-CDs with the unique characteristics of different metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), these probes not only achieved a multi-signal fluorescence response to TCs but also ingeniously incorporated a visual colorimetric function. This work not only offered a novel and efficient strategy for on-site quantitative analysis of TCs, but also demonstrated the development of a three-channel ratiometric fluorescence/visual colorimetric sensor array. Based on a multi-channel strategy that integrates both increasing and decreasing signals, all three probes generated multi-signal fluorescence responses for on-site quantitative analysis of TCs. These responses were accompanied by distinct color transitions (blue to green, blue to red, yellow to red) under 365 nm UV light. This design not only improved detection accuracy but also enhanced result intuitiveness, enabling rapid assessment of TC presence via color changes. Additionally, to improve detection convenience and practicality, a handheld portable 3D-printed platform was introduced. This platform leveraged the probes' unique ratiometric fluorescence and visual responses to generate distinct optical 'fingerprints' for each TC, facilitating the detection and identification of both individual TCs and their mixtures. This method simplifies the detection process, improves detection efficiency, and makes on-site detection faster and more convenient. Additionally, Hierarchical Cluster Analysis (HCA) and Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) further enhanced the recognition ability and accuracy of the sensor array. This method provides new insights into future research on the detection of TCs and holds promise for promoting its widespread application in fields such as food safety, environmental monitoring, and clinical medicine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental sections
Materials and reagents
ce:section.ce:para: All chemical reagents were at least analytical grade. EuCl3·6H2O, citric acid, Mg(OH)2, Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, 1,4-Benzenedicarboxylic acid (H2BDC), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), formamide, 3,5-dicarboxyphenylboronic acid (BBDC), rhodamine 6G, tetracycline, oxytetracycline, chlortetracycline, metacycline, doxycycline, and doxycycline hyclate were purchased from Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Instruments and equipment
ce:section.ce:para: The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained on a Verios 5UC electron microscope (Thermo Fisher, USA). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEM-ARM200F electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured by Spectro-Type FTIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, America). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were tested on the D8 DISCOVER diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was recorded on Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The fluorescence lifetime and photoluminescence spectra were conducted by a FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, Britain) with an integrating sphere and LS55 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) respectively. UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on a Lambda 35 UV analyzer (PerkinElmer, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Mg,N-CDs, Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of Mg,N-CDs was relied on the previous reports [32]. In brief, 0.25 g of citric acid was dissolved in 20 ml of ultrapure water. Then, 0.105 g of Mg(OH)2 and 5 ml of ethylenediamine were slowly added under continuous stirring to form a homogeneous solution. After that, the solution was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave reactor and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. Finally, the yellow products were purified by dialysis.

The Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5 were prepared according to the literature with slight modifications [33]. 0.0439 g of Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O and 0.0168 g of H2BDC were dissolved in 8 mL of DMF and kept stirring for 5 min. Subsequently, 2 mL of Mg,N-CDs solution was added to the above solution. The mixture was then vigorously stirred at room temperature for 30 min. The solid sample was collected by centrifugation and washed using DMF and ethanol to remove any unreacted reagents, followed by drying at 60 °C under vacuum for 24 h.

The synthesis of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs was performed based on a hydrothermal method with some modifications [34]. Specifically, 0.0258 g of EuCl3·6H2O and 0.021 g of BBDC were dissolved in a mixture of 7 mL of DMF. After that, and 3 mL of Mg,N-CDs solution was added to the above solution. The mixture was then vigorously stirred at room temperature for 2 h. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 130 °C for 12 h. The solid orange-yellow powder was collected by centrifugation and washed using DMF and ethanol, followed by drying at 60 °C under vacuum for 24 h.

The synthesis of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G involved the dissolution of 10 mg of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs and 10 mg of Rhodamine-6G in 10 ml of formamide solution, followed by vigorous stirring for 3 h. The product was collected by centrifugation and washed using DMF and ethanol, followed by drying at 60 °C under vacuum for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of ratiometric fluorescence sensor arrays for TCs
ce:section.ce:para: Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G were employed as the three sensing units to construct sensor arrays for TCs. The concentration of the synthesized probe of the prepared probe was 0.1 mg/mL. Then, different concentration of TCs were added to the above fluorescence probs, respectively. The ratiometric fluorescence value of Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5 (F525/F440), Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs (F615/F440) and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G (F615/F552) were employed as indicator for TCs under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. This process is repeated six times for each TC concentration, resulting in 7 concentration × 3 arrays × 6 replicates of training data. Subsequently, the mixture is transferred to a 24-well plate. Then, these data were treated by LDA and HCA for detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of colorimetric sensor arrays for TCs
ce:section.ce:para: The portable detector was design and constructed for colorimetric sensor arrays utilizing SolidWorks software and 3D printing technology. The colorimetric sensor array detector was composed of a 3D-printed box, a 365 nm ultraviolet light source, and a smartphone equipped with a color recognition APP. The portable detectors captured the fluorescence colors of three sensing units using the APP of Colorpicker on a smartphone and converted them into RGB values, whose B/R values were linearly related to the concentration of TCs. The G/B value of the Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, the R/(G + B) value of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, and the R/(G + B) value of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G as the three signaling indicators for the colorimetric sensor array were linearly related to the concentration of TCs. This process generated 6 TCs × 3 arrays × 6 replicates of training data for each of the six types of TC. The fluorescent color information was extracted from the images and then processed with LDA or HCA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of multiple TCs
ce:section.ce:para: To further assess the analytical performance of the sensor array, the dual-model array was used to discriminate various mixtures of TCs. By keeping the total concentration of TCs at 20 μM or 50 μM, the sensor array was used to discriminate different combinations of three, four or even five TCs with various proportion of concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The anti-interference abilities of the sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the anti-interference capabilities of the sensor array, various types of solutions were used as the solvents instead of ultrapure water, such as cationic solutions (a mixture of 10 μM each of K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+ and Fe3+), anionic solutions (a mixture of 10 μM each of Cl−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, NO3 − and acetate), and protein solutions (a mixture of 1 mg/L each of ovalbumin and bovine serum albumin).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing and discrimination of TCs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The method described in Chapter 2.4 and 2.5 was used for the sensing and discrimination of TCs in three actual samples of tap water, urine and milk. The water sample was tap water from Wuhan city. The milk was purchased from a local supermarket. The urine samples were provided by student volunteers from North China University of Science and Technology and the sampling unit was the affiliated hospital of North China University of Science and Technology. The milk samples (2 mL), urine samples (5 mL), and tap water samples (8 mL) were mixed with 0.4 mL of 0.2 M Na2EDTA and 0.6 mL of methanol in 10 mL centrifuge tubes. Following centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, the samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. Then the samples were diluted to 100-fold for further use [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Preparation and characterization of three sensing units
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis scheme of three sensing units (Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G) are displayed in Fig. 1 A. The three sensing units exhibited distinct colors under natural light: white for Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, pale yellow for Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, and orange-red for Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G. However, under a 365 nm UV lamp, these sensing units showed three different colors, such as bright blue (Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5), red (Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs), and yellow (Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G), respectively (Fig. S1 in the ESI). The characterization of Mg,N-CDs are displayed in Fig. S2. Fig. 1B showed the XPS analysis of the Mg,N-CDs and the three sensing units. Mg,N-CDs contain four elements of C, N, O, and Mg (Black curve in Fig. 1B and Fig. S2A–S2D in the ESI), which still existed in that of three sensing units (Fig. 1B). Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5 exhibited characteristic peaks of Zn 2p at 1020.78 eV and 1043.74 eV(Red curve in Fig. 1B and Fig. S3 in the ESI). Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs displayed characteristic peaks of Eu 3d at 1134.96 eV and 1164.56 eV(Blue curve in Fig. 1B and Fig. S4 in the ESI). Similarly, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G showed characteristic peaks of Eu 3d at 1135.12 eV and 1164.68 eV (Green curve in Fig. 2 B and Fig. S5 in the ESI). The size and shape of the Mg,N-CDs and the three sensing units were investigated by TEM image and SEM image (Fig. 1C–F). The TEM image of Mg,N-CDs showed that the nanoparticles are monodisperse spherical with an average size of 1.3 nm (Fig. 1C). After encapsulated by the MOF-5, the Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5 exhibited a rhombic structure, confirming the presence of crystal plane growth (Fig. 1D). After embedded in the Eu-MOFs, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs has a rough spherical structure with a particle size of approximately 300–500 nm (Fig. 1E). Compared with Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOF, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G exhibited an increased particle size to 0.8–1 μm (Fig. 1F). The EDS elemental mappings further revealed the element distribution in Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G, which demonstrated the synthesis of three sensing units (Fig. 1G–K and Fig. S6 and S7 in the ESI). As shown in Fig. 1L, the characteristic absorption peaks corresponding to N–H, O–H, –COO, and C–N of Mg,N-CDs are observed in that of three sensing units. More importantly, Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5 exhibited a characteristic absorption band at 755 cm−1 belonging to the Zn–O vibration of MOF-5, as well as characteristic absorption bands at 1506 cm−1 and 1315 cm−1 attributed to C–O vibrations (Green curve in Fig. 1L). Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs (Red curve in Fig. 1L) and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G (Black curve in Fig. 1L) showed characteristic absorption peaks at 1378 cm−1 and 1387 cm−1, respectively, corresponding to the B–O stretching vibration of Eu-MOFs. Furthermore, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G displayed a characteristic absorption peak at 1687 cm−1 belonging to the C=O vibration of Rh-6G. What is more, Mg,N-CDs exhibited an amorphous structure with a broad peak at 2θ = 26° (Fig. S2E in the ESI). The characteristic diffraction peaks (6.8°, 9.7°, 13.7°, and 15.5°) of MOF-5 were observed in the spectra of Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5 (Fig. S8A). The XRD patterns of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G were consistent well with the pattern (8.8°, 10.6°, 18.5°, and 20°) of Eu-MOFs, indicating that the crystal structures of them remain unchanged in the presence of Mg,N-CDs and Rh-6G (Fig. 1M) [32–38].

The optical Characteristics of the Mg,N-CDs and three sensing units were also investigated. The UV absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of Mg,N-CDs were displayed in Fig. S2F. The Mg,N-CDs exhibited a sharp absorption peak at 230 nm due to the aromatic π-π∗ transition, while the absorption peak at 350 nm was originated from the n-π∗ transition of C=O and C=N groups on the surface of Mg,N-CDs. The Mg,N-CDs had strong fluorescence emission at 440 nm with the excitation at 350 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield of Mg,N-CDs was calculated to be 9.22 %. The fluorescence emission of the Mg,N-CDs was excitation-independent (Fig. S2G in the ESI). As shown in Fig. 1N, both of MOF-5 and Eu-MOFs displayed no fluorescence emission. After coating on the surface of Mg,N-CDs, the fluorescence spectra of Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5 and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs showed the strong emission peak at 440 nm from Mg,N-CDs. In contrast, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G exhibited a decreased fluorescence emission at 440 nm and a new emission peak at 552 nm belonged to Rh-6G. The emission spectra were investigated under excitation from 330 nm to 390 nm. Fluorescence lifetime of Mg,N-CDs, Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G were 19.14 ns, 1.63 μs, 83.13 μs and 123.38 μs, respectively (Fig. S8B in the ESI). The fluorescence lifetime of Mg,N-CDs increased with the protection of the MOFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility of ratiometric fluorescence sensor array for TCs
ce:section.ce:para: As one typical kind of the TCs, the fluorescence response of Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G to TC were analyzed (Figs. S8C, D, E in the ESI). With the addition of TC, the fluorescence emission of Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5 at 440 nm was quenched and a new emission peak at 525 nm appeared (Fig. S8C in the ESI). Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs had the similar quenching of fluorescence emission at 440 nm and increased fluorescence emission at 615 nm in the present of TC (Fig. S8D in the ESI). Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G also displayed different fluorescence response to TC. including the fluorescence quenching both at 440 nm and 552 nm while increasing at 615 nm (Fig. S8E in the ESI).

The discussion of the fluorescence response mechanism between three sensing units and TC is crucial for the development of sensor arrays for TCs with the similar structures. The significant overlap between the excitation spectra of Mg,N-CDs absorption spectra of TC suggested that the inner filter effect (IFE) could be a possible mechanism for the fluorescence quenching at 440 nm in the three sensing units (Fig. S2H in the ESI) [39]. The fluorescence lifetimes of Mg,N-CDs in the absence and present of TC were 19.14 ns and 19.81 ns, respectively (Fig. S2I in the ESI). The unchanged fluorescence lifetime was further confirmed the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the IEF.

The coordination between TC and metal ions played a key role in the enhancement of fluorescence emission in the three sensing units [40,41]. As shown in Fig. S8C, significant fluorescence peaks at 525 nm emerged only when TC was combined with Zn2+, MOF-5, and Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5 through the antenna effect (AE). Similarly, upon the addition of TC, the enhancement of fluorescence emission at 615 nm in Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G was also attributed to the chelation of Eu3+ with TC through the AE (Fig. S8D, E in the ESI). The absorption peaks of Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G were increased with significant red shift, which further demonstrated the AE between TC and metal ions (Fig. S9 in the ESI). The SEM and TEM analysis of the three sensing units before and after the addition of TC were displayed in Fig. 2A–F. The structure of Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5 collapsed after the addition of TC due to the binding of Zn2+ with TC (Fig. 2A vs. Fig. 2D). Meanwhile, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs exhibited a noticeable platelet-like structure and increased particle size after the addition of TC, confirming the formation of Eu3+-TC complexes (Fig. 2B vs. Fig. 2E). Additionally, the surface of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G became smoother, indicating the detachment of adsorbed Rh-6G and its interaction with TC (Fig. 2C vs. Fig. 2F). Therefore, IFE and AE were the main fluorescence response mechanism between three sensing units and TC [39–44].

To investigate the feasibility of the sensor array to identification of TCs, six TCs with similar structure such as TC, OTC, CTC, MTC, DTC and DOX (Fig. S10 in the ESI) were employed to evaluate the recognition ability of three sensing units. The detailed effects of six TCs (TC, OTC, CTC, MTC, DTC and DOX) (30 μM) on the fluorescence performance of the three probes were displayed in Fig. 2G–I and Fig. S11-13. In brief, all three sensing units exhibited significant changes to different degrees in response to the six TCs, due to the different AE between the sensing unit and TCs (Fig. 2G–I). Then, the obtained fluorescence intensities were collected for producing pattern (F525/F440 for sensing unit of Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, F615/F440 for sensing unit of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, F615/F552 for sensing unit of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G) as parameters for ratiometric fluorescence detection of TCs(Fig. S11–13 in the ESI). Especially, the color difference based on the extracted R, G, and B values also intuitively confirmed the excellent visual discrimination performance. However, no notable effects were observed for other antibiotics such as amoxicillin (AMX), chloramphenicol (CHL), roxithromycin (RTM), kanamycin (KAN), sulfadiazine (SD), cefixime (CFM), and pefloxacin (PEF) (Fig. S14 in the ESI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of TCs by the dual-model sensor array using three sensing units
ce:section.ce:para: The quantitative measurements were conducted for each of the six TCs by the dual-model sensor using three sensing units respectively, using TC as a typical representative for illustration (Fig. S15–20 in the ESI). With the addition of TC, Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5 exhibited a significant increase in fluorescence emission at 525 nm and a decreased emission at 440 nm (Fig. S15A in the ESI). The ratio of emission intensity at 525 nm (F525) to that at 440 nm (F440) exhibited a linear relationship with the TC concentration within the range of 0–70 μM (Fig. S15B in the ESI). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 7.3 nM (The LOD is determined according to the 3σ/m criterion, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank or the standard deviation of the intercept, and m is the slope of the calibration plot [45]). The fluorescence color of Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5 changed from blue to green with the addition of TC. The fluorescent color images of solution were acquired and extracted, which could be used to establish a linear relationship between G/B value and the concentration of TC (Fig. S15C in the ESI). Similarly, the ratio of emission intensity at 615 nm (F615) to that at 440 nm (F440) of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs showed a linear relationship with the TC concentration within the range of 0–70 μM with the LOD of 4.7 nM (Fig. S15D and 15E in the ESI). The fluorescence color of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs showed a significant change from blue to red with the addition of TC. R/(G + B) value of the fluorescence image displayed a linear relationship to the concentration of TC (Fig. S15F in the ESI). Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G exhibited a notable increase in fluorescence emission at 615 nm with the addition of TC, while the emission at 440 nm and 552 nm gradually decreased with the increased concentration of TC (Fig. S15G in the ESI). The ratio of emission intensity at 615 nm (F615) to that at 552 nm (F552) of Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G gave a linear relationship with the TC concentration within the range of 0–70 μM with a LOD of 7.0 nM (Fig. S15H in the ESI). The R/(G + B) value of the fluorescence image from Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G exhibited a linear relationship to the concentration of TC, along with the color changing from green to orange (Fig. S15I in the ESI). Due to the different binding affinities of TC (Fig. S15 in the ESI), OTC (Fig. S16 in the ESI), CTC (Fig. S17 in the ESI), MTC (Fig. S18 in the ESI), DTC (Fig. S19 in the ESI), and DOX (Fig. S20 in the ESI) towards the three sensing units, competitive absorption of excitation energy induces differentiated response characteristics in the IFE, further leading to the changes in fluorescence intensity varied (Table S1 in the ESI), resulting in different colors displayed by the probe solutions upon exposure to different TCs. Compared with other developed optical sensor array (Table S2 in the ESI), our dual-model sensor array based on three types of multichannel ratiometric fluorescent probes (Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, and Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G) shows better discrimination performance for the analysis of multiple TCs.

Because dual-model system provides numerous advantages, including comprehensive information, improved sensitivity and specificity, enhanced resolution, and broader application range, a handheld portable colorimetric sensor array platform was also established utilizing 3D printing technology (Fig. S21 in the ESI). The fluorescence images of the different sensing units with each type of TC (30 μM) are displayed in Fig. 3 A. The distinguishable fluorescence color in the presence of different TCs, demonstrating the excellent visual discrimination performance. This 3D printing device comprised of a 365 nm UV lamp, a 24-well plate, a smartphone equipped with an RGB color recognizer, and a 3D printed black box. The black box ensured consistency of the detection environment (e.g., camera height, light source angle, darkness). The smartphone was fixed at an optimal angle and height on the black box for capturing color from sensing elements (Fig. 3B and Fig. S21 in the ESI).

Therefore, the sensor array for detecting and differentiating various TCs could be further established both in ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric modes (Fig. 3C). The combination of ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric sensing can provide enhanced sensitivity, specificity, and reliability for the detection of TC, making it a promising tool for environmental monitoring, food safety, and clinical diagnostics [14,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification and characterization of individual TCs
ce:section.ce:para: Benefiting from the excellent fluorescence properties, the ratio of F525/F440 for Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, the ratio of F615/F440 for Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, and the ratio of F615/F552 for Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G were used as the three signal indicators for the ratiometric fluorescence sensor array. Similarly, the ratio of G/B for Mg,N-CDs/MOF-5, the ratio of R/(G + B) for Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs, and the ratio of R/(G + B) for Mg,N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@Rh-6G were served as the three signal indicators for the colorimetric sensor array. These two sets of indicators form the basis for two distinct proportional logic operation systems, one for ratiometric fluorescence and the other for colorimetric analysis.

The ratiometric fluorescence array (7 concentrations × 3 arrays × 6 replicate training data) was analyzed by LDA. Given the significant contributions of the first and second most important discriminant functions among the three units, a two-dimensional score plot was generated using these two discriminant functions for visualization [47,48]. Additionally, since the first discriminant function contributes the most significantly, its relationship with TCs concentration was further explored. The TC with seven different concentrations were effectively distinguished into separate categories in the ratiometric fluorescence sensor array (Fig. S22A in the ESI). There is a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.994) between the first important discriminant function and TCs concentration in the range of 0–60 μM in the ratiometric fluorescence sensor array (Fig. S22B in the ESI). Similarly, Fig. S22C (in the ESI) showed that the seven concentrations of TCs were also separated into distinct categories in the colorimetric sensor array by handheld portable platform. A good linear relationship between the first important discriminant function and TC concentration was also acquired in the colorimetric sensor array (Fig. S22D in the ESI). Through data analysis and visualization using LDA, we found that OTC (Fig. S23 in the ESI), CTC (Fig. S24 in the ESI), MTC (Fig. S25 in the ESI), DTC (Fig. S26 in the ESI), and DOX (Fig. S27 in the ESI) could all be effectively distinguished into separate categories in the dual-modal sensor array.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification and characterization of multiple TCs
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric fluorescence and corresponding fluorescence color variation gave the distinguishable fluorescence signal and fluorescence color in the presence of six TCs, making the use of this dual-modal sensor array for TC recognition feasible. Based on this observation, the dual-modal sensor array was employed to record the fluorescence spectra and RGB information of different TCs at three concentration levels (10 μM, 40 μM, 60 μM) using the three units. Based on this observation, the dual-modal sensor array was employed to record the fluorescence spectra and RGB information of different TCs at three concentration levels (10 μM, 40 μM, 60 μM) using the three probes. First, linear discriminant analysis (LDA) was employed to convert the obtained training matrix (consisting of 6 TCs × 3 arrays × 6 repeated training data) into three canonical scores and a two-dimensional score plot was generated using the first and second most important discriminant functions. As shown in Fig. S28A–C (in the ESI) and Fig. 4 A–C, the six different TCs are clearly distinguished into six separate categories without any overlap. Then hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) (Fig. S28D–F in the ESI and Fig. 4D–F) showed that the six types of TCs could be clearly distinguished without any cross-effects, demonstrating the excellent TC recognition performance of this dual-model sensor array.

Given that TCs often coexisted in real scenarios, simultaneous detection and analysis of multiple TCs was essential [24,49]. The homemade dual-model sensor array was utilized to differentiate various mixtures of multiple TCs, with the total antibiotic concentration maintained at 20 μM and 50 μM. Initially, two antibiotics (TC and OTC, CTC, or MTC) were distinguished at varying molar ratios (Fig. S28G–I in the ESI and Fig. 4G–I). We found that under the dual-model conditions, the mixtures of TCs with different molar ratios at both concentrations were well-classified without overlap. Because of the excellent recognition performance for binary combinations of TCs, we further expanded the analysis to ternary combinations (TC, OTC, and CTC), quaternary combinations (TC, OTC, CTC, and MTC), and even quinary combinations (TC, OTC, CTC, MTC, and DTC) at varying proportions. As depicted in Fig. S28J–L and S29 in the ESI and Fig. 4J–L, the results showed that this dual-model sensor array platform was able to fully distinguish different combinations at various levels under both ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric modes, indicating the robust discrimination capability for mixtures of different TCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The anti-interference capability of the sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-interference capability of the sensor array is crucial to the accuracy of detection [47–50]. To evaluate the anti-interference capability,we tested the discrimination of different TCs at varying concentration levels (20 μM and 50 μM) in various complex matrices (Fig. 5 and Fig. S30–S32 in the ESI). As shown in Fig. 5A and B, the TCs were successfully distinguished into separate categories even in solutions containing multiple anions or cations, demonstrating the robust anti-ionic interference ability of the homemade dual-modal sensor array platform. To mimic the potential presence of protein components in real matrices, further tests were conducted with TCs in a mixture of 0.1 g/L ovalbumin and bovine serum albumin. As illustrated in Fig. 5C, the platform maintained its excellent recognition performance. Due to the good anti-interference properties of the platform, we proceeded with discrimination experiments in several real matrices (tap water, milk, and urine). The preprocessed real matrices exhibited minimal fluorescence and did not significantly alter the signals of the three probes. The results (Fig. 5D–F) indicated that the dual-model sensor array platform could still fully distinguish the six different TCs in all three real matrices. Therefore, this dual-model sensor array platform possesses excellent anti-interference capabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of TCs in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To verify whether the sensor array could distinguish samples containing unknown components, a “double-blind” principle was employed. 50 blind samples were prepared by randomly combining the six types of tetracyclines (TCs). All these unknown samples were accurately identified, with 100 % correct classification (Tables S3 and S4). Subsequently, 20 unknown antibiotic samples at a concentration of 20 μM were further classified in urine solutions. As shown in Tables S5 and S6, these samples were also accurately identified, with 100 % correct classification. The excellent recognition efficiency and accuracy validate the reliability of the sensor array for detecting and identifying TCs in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, we designed three multi-channel ratiometric fluorescence probes, fabricated a handheld portable platform, and developed a dual-model sensor array for the detection and identification of multiple TCs in actual samples. The different structure of TCs endowed their various interactions with three sensing elements, resulting in different fluorescence and color responses, which were collected and analyzed by HCA and LDA for the identification of six TCs with high selectivity and sensitivity. This method is a more accurate, reliable, low-cost, high-performance detection tool for the detection and identification of TCs, opening a new way for the identification of multiple emerging contaminants in environments.
